# The Global Impact of Public Health Policies

![Kent-BSPH-Blog-Global-Impact--Public-Health-Policies.jpg](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMjNweCIgd2lkdGg9IjgwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=)

Health means fair and equal access to care so that everyone can achieve their highest level of health.1 It incorporates not just life-saving care, but also preventative care. However, social and economic obstacles can prevent some minority populations from accessing the care they need, leading to global health disparities.

Public health policies are essential building blocks of better health. Along with research and practical health programs, implementing health policies can help break down socioeconomic barriers and allow broader access to healthcare.2 Below, we’ll discuss some examples of public health policies and their [impact on health around the world](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/college-of-public-health/community/the-impact-of-healthcare-policy-on-public-health-outcomes). We’ll also unpack some of the biggest challenges to promoting health and strategies to overcome them.

## Understanding Social Determinants of Health

To create effective policies that [promote health](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/college-of-public-health/community/promoting-health-across-borders), governing bodies must understand health barriers. Understanding social determinants of health, including environmental pollution, discrimination and housing, can help governments shape effective health policies. The Office of the Assistant Secretary for Health (OASH) for the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services groups social determinants of health into five domains:5

## How Public Health Policies Promote Health

Public health policies can directly or indirectly impact wellness across a population. For example, consider policies implemented to stop the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic, such as mandated masking, social distancing, and travel bans.3 Public health policies that promote health may not have such a direct impact but can encourage greater access to healthcare over time.

Here are a few relevant policy examples:

### Universal Health Coverage

Universal health coverage tackles one of the most immediate barriers to healthcare: affordability. With universal coverage, individuals can access insurance regardless of their income or employment status. In the United States, the Affordable Care Act is an example of a policy that promotes greater access to healthcare without reducing the quality of care.4

### Health Promotion and Disease Prevention

Health promotion and disease prevention policies help to stop the spread of diseases that may affect some populations more than others. Again, COVID-19 policies are a good example. Other examples include handwashing regulations in food service businesses and mandated sex education in schools.

### Access to Medicines and Vaccines

Improved access to medicines and vaccines, particularly in rural areas, can prevent long-term health conditions and help patients avoid more expensive, challenging care in the future.

## Effective Public Health Policies By Region

Health disparities exist around the world. In high-income countries, health is influenced by the welfare state, or the government allocation of public resources and services. Research suggests that there is less health in countries with an extensive welfare state, such as Norway and Sweden.6 However, that doesn’t necessarily mean the policies of Nordic countries can be applied everywhere. Effective policies must address the needs and disparities of the region where they are being implemented.

For example, in rural, high-poverty areas of the global south, disease prevention programs and access to vaccines may help reduce geographical health disparities. These policies are often implemented by international organizations, including the World Health Organization, which funds research and evidence-based programs to improve health in low-income nations.7

### Case Study: Free Access to Retroviral Therapy in South Africa

In South Africa, as in the U.S., the AIDS epidemic has disproportionately affected Black populations. In 2004, the South African government began offering free retroviral therapy to all HIV-positive patients, regardless of income or health status, to counter this massive health disparity.

Since the program began, annual mortality rates in HIV-positive patients have been reduced by 27%, with outcomes for Black Africans aged 25-49 most significantly impacted.8 This is an example of a [public health policy](https://onlinedegrees.kent.edu/college-of-public-health/community/the-impact-of-healthcare-policy-on-public-health-outcomes) that can improve health by addressing an issue primarily affecting minority populations.

## Challenges in Promoting Health Through Public Health Policies

Public health policies are crucial to countering global health disparities but aren’t always easy to implement. These are among the most notable challenges to effective policy change:

### Strategies for Overcoming Challenges

It is possible to overcome and promote better population health through public health policies. Strategies that can help create targeted, practical public policy include:

## The Ongoing Process of Creating Better Public Health Policies

Creating better public health policies is an ongoing process. While it takes advocacy, intervention, and a lot of education, implementing effective public health policies will improve health worldwide. As global demographics, challenges, and health concerns change, policies will have to change with them. Continued research and advocacy will always be necessary, so don’t hesitate to learn more.

Social Determinants of Health
=============================

![Image 1: HP2030 logo](https://odphp.health.gov/themes/custom/hp2030/images/hp2030-logo-lb.svg)

What are social determinants of health?
---------------------------------------

Social determinants of health (SDOH) are the conditions in the environments where people are born, live, learn, work, play, worship, and age that affect a wide range of health, functioning, and quality-of-life outcomes and risks.

SDOH can be grouped into 5 domains:

[Economic Stability](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/topic/economic-stability)

[Education Access and Quality](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/topic/education-access-and-quality)

[Health Care Access and Quality](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/topic/health-care-access-and-quality)

[Neighborhood and Built Environment](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/topic/neighborhood-and-built-environment)

[Social and Community Context](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/topic/social-and-community-context)

Image

![Image 2](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2020-07/SDOH-NoText_0.svg)

[Download SDOH graphic](https://odphp.health.gov/sites/default/files/2020-10/Healthy%20People%202030%20SDOH%20Graphics.zip "Healthy People 2030 SDOH Graphics.zip") (141.23 KB) 

### Suggested citation

Healthy People 2030, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Disease Prevention and Health Promotion. Retrieved [date graphic was accessed], from https://health.gov/healthypeople/objectives-and-data/social-determinants-health

Copy Citation

Social determinants of health (SDOH) have a major impact on people’s health, well-being, and quality of life. Examples of SDOH include:

*   Safe housing, transportation, and neighborhoods
*   Education, job opportunities, and income
*   Access to nutritious foods and physical activity opportunities
*   Discrimination and violence
*   Polluted air and water
*   Language and literacy skills

SDOH also contribute to wide health disparities and inequities. For example, people who don't have access to grocery stores with healthy foods are less likely to have good nutrition. That raises their risk of health conditions like heart disease, diabetes, and obesity — and even lowers life expectancy relative to people who do have access to healthy foods.

Just promoting healthy choices won't eliminate these and other health disparities. Instead, public health organizations and their partners in sectors like education, transportation, and housing need to take action to improve the conditions in people's environments.

That's why Healthy People 2030 has an increased and overarching focus on SDOH.

How Does Healthy People 2030 Address SDOH?
------------------------------------------

One of Healthy People 2030’s 5 overarching goals is specifically related to SDOH: “Create social, physical, and economic environments that promote attaining the full potential for health and well-being for all.”

In line with this goal, Healthy People 2030 features many objectives related to SDOH. These objectives highlight the importance of "upstream" factors— usually unrelated to health care delivery — in improving health and reducing health disparities.

More than a dozen workgroups made up of subject matter experts with different backgrounds and areas of expertise developed these objectives. One of these groups, the [Social Determinants of Health Workgroup](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/about/workgroups/social-determinants-health), focuses solely on SDOH.

Explore Research Related to SDOH
--------------------------------

Social determinants of health affect nearly everyone in one way or another. Our literature summaries provide a snapshot of the latest research related to specific SDOH.

[Read SDOH literature summaries](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/priority-areas/social-determinants-health/literature-summaries)

View SDOH Infographics
----------------------

Each SDOH infographic represents a single example from each of the 5 domains of the social determinants of health. You can download them, print them, and share them with your networks.

[Check out SDOH infographics](https://odphp.health.gov/healthypeople/priority-areas/social-determinants-health/literature-summaries#block-sdohinfographics)

Learn How SDOH Affect Older Adults
----------------------------------

SDOH have a big impact on our chances of staying healthy as we age. Healthy People’s actionable scenarios highlight ways professionals can support older adults’ health and well-being.

[Get tips to improve SDOH for older adults](https://odphp.health.gov/our-work/national-health-initiatives/healthy-aging/social-determinants-health-and-older-adults)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Effective public health policies addressing social determinants are crucial. Policies like Health in All Policies (HiAP) can create systemic change and improve health outcomes globally.
Engaging communities in health initiatives ensures that policies reflect local needs, fostering trust and increasing participation in health-promoting activities and resources.
Addressing health disparities through targeted policies and interventions can promote health equity, ensuring all populations enjoy equal access to healthcare and essential resources.
